claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions.

**First, let me ask about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child? This will help me understand which eligibility pathway applies:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16 and 19 years old
- 20 years old or over

Please tell me the child's age (or age range), and if you have multiple children you'd like to ask about, please let me know!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me work through this assessment for Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), they automatically qualify in terms of age. Now I need to check a few more requirements.

**Next question:** Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

(For an 8-year-old, this is typically straightforward - most children this age are not in paid work, so the answer would be "yes" - they are working less than 24 hours per week or not working at all.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes perfect sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, is Alex receiving any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own name? For most 8-year-olds, they wouldn't be claiming these benefits themselves, so the answer would typically be "yes" (meaning he is NOT receiving these benefits).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This would include things like:
- Apprenticeships in England (any level)
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland
- Apprenticeships in Wales (except Foundation level)

For an 8-year-old, this wouldn't apply, but I need to confirm. Is Alex in an apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 8, he's still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes complete sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to ask about **your** circumstances as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You would meet these requirements if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Do any of these apply to you? The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned that Alex lives with you at home, which means you satisfy the responsibility requirement. But just to confirm - do ANY of these apply:

1. **You live with the child** (which you've already told me is the case!)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you've already told me Alex lives with you at home in Manchester, that clearly satisfies the responsibility requirement. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, though you can transfer the claim if both parties agree.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

- Is Alex currently in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex being fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you (which is what you've indicated)?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct, or is there any special circumstance I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in hospital for a few months now. But he's still my child and I'm asking about whether I can claim for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that important information. I understand this must be a difficult time for you. Let me check the rules for when a child is in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. There are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in hospital. Let me ask you about this:

**How long has Alex been in hospital?**

The rule is that Child Benefit can continue for up to 12 weeks while a child is in hospital or residential accommodation. The time is counted cumulatively - meaning if Alex has been in hospital multiple times with gaps of less than 28 days between stays, those periods are added together.

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for "a few months now." Can you tell me:
1. How many weeks (approximately) has Alex been in hospital?
2. Has this been one continuous stay, or has he been in and out of hospital?

This will help me determine which rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for about 12 weeks now - it's been one continuous stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. So Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks in one continuous stay. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - at 12 weeks, we're right at the limit. However, there's an important exception to be aware of:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he is in hospital?**

This is important because even when a child has been in hospital for 12 weeks or more, you can still claim Child Benefit if you're regularly spending money on them. This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or personal items for them
- Travel costs to visit them
- Treats or gifts
- Any other regular expenses for Alex's benefit

Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy him clothes, magazines, and his favorite food to bring when I visit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's wonderful that you're staying so involved in Alex's care. Your regular spending on him is important here. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now, one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's care, accommodation, or maintenance while he's in hospital? 

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child. So I need to know:
- Is the local council paying for Alex's hospital care or accommodation?
- Or is his care being covered by the NHS/other means without local council funding?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying for anything - it's just the NHS hospital care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's the final piece of information I needed. Let me now provide you with the outcome of this assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Good News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit** ✓

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** for Child Benefit for Alex.

### **Why You're Eligible:**

✓ **Alex is 8 years old** - under 16, so he automatically qualifies by age  
✓ **Not in paid work** - he doesn't work 24+ hours per week  
✓ **Not receiving benefits** - Alex isn't claiming benefits in his own right  
✓ **Not in an apprenticeship** - he's in primary school  
✓ **You meet residency requirements** - you live in the UK (Manchester)  
✓ **You're responsible for Alex** - he normally lives with you at home  
✓ **No competing claim** - nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✓ **Hospital stay exception applies** - even though Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, the fact that you're regularly spending money on him (buying clothes, magazines, and his favorite food) means you can continue to claim Child Benefit  
✓ **Council not paying** - the local council isn't paying for Alex's care (it's NHS care)

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks

### **Important Note About Hospital Stays:**

Because you're regularly spending money on Alex, you can continue to claim beyond the standard 12-week hospital limit. Just make sure you keep doing this to maintain eligibility.

**More information:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers soon. Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16, so automatically qualifies by age). He is not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying residency requirements. The claimant has responsibility for Alex as they live together. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means the exception to the 12-week hospital rule applies. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is provided by the NHS. Therefore, all eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of around 12 weeks, the claimant's regular spending on Alex allows the claim to continue beyond the standard 12-week hospital limit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16, so automatically qualifies by age). He is not in paid work, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying residency requirements. The claimant has responsibility for Alex as they live together. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food), which means the exception to the 12-week hospital rule applies. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is provided by the NHS. Therefore, all eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of around 12 weeks, the claimant's regular spending on Alex allows the claim to continue beyond the standard 12-week hospital limit.
